title: Detecting Exciton Condensation through Charge Transport in Semiconductor Heterostructures
authors: Caterina Zerba, Léo Mangeolle, Michael Knap
abstract: Direct evidence of exciton condensation in semiconductor heterostructures remains elusive. Here we propose charge transport of doped carriers as a probe of exciton condensation in transition-metal dichalcogenide heterostructures and identify distinct experimental signatures. First, condensation suppresses the phase space for carrier scattering, leading to a reduction in resistivity, that provides a general diagnostic of exciton condensation. Second, in heterostructures with a tunable solid-state Feshbach resonance, condensate-induced hybridization between doped carriers and trion bound states qualitatively modifies transport. In particular, near resonance, this hybridization yields a negative effective mass and a corresponding sign reversal of the Hall resistivity. These results establish charge transport as a promising route for detecting and characterizing exciton condensation in semiconductor heterostructures.
summary: The paper proposes using charge transport measurements to detect exciton condensation in semiconductor heterostructures. By modeling the system as an interacting Bose-Fermi mixture tunable via a Feshbach resonance, the authors predict two distinct signatures: a general resistivity drop and a highly characteristic sign reversal in the Hall resistivity ($ho_{hxy}$). This establishes charge transport as a powerful diagnostic tool for studying excitonic quantum phases.
main_problem: The primary goal is to find a measurable signature of exciton condensation in semiconductor heterostructures using charge transport measurements.
main_result: Two key signatures are established: (1) A general reduction in resistivity due to suppressed carrier scattering, and (2) A striking sign reversal in the Hall resistivity ($ho_{hxy}$) when a tunable solid-state Feshbach resonance is present.
method: The analysis employs kinetic theory by deriving and solving coupled Boltzmann equations for three quasiparticle species near an avoided crossing induced by exciton-carrier hybridization.
model_or_system: The system models strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures in semiconductor heterostructures, specifically using Transition Metal Dichalcogenide (TMD) layers. The interactions are tunable via a solid-state Feshbach resonance, leading to the formation of hybridized fermionic bands.
key_observables: Resistivity ($ho_{hxx}, ho_{hxy}$), particularly focusing on the sign reversal and magnitude changes in $ho_{hxy}$.
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), detuning ($\Delta$) relative to resonance, and coupling strengths ($g$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mean-field treatments for condensation, a kinetic theory approach using coupled Boltzmann equations, and approximations regarding the magnetic field regimes (small vs. large $B$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate $ho_{hxy}$ versus detuning ($\Delta$) showing the characteristic sign reversal upon condensation, and $ho_{h}^{xx}$ showing peak narrowing with increasing condensate density.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the physical problem to developing a theoretical framework using coupled Boltzmann equations. It analyzes different limits (small/large B) and derives specific diagnostic signatures based on band reconstruction and scattering phase space changes.
why_interesting: This work bridges concepts from ultracold atomic physics (Feshbach resonance, BEC diagnostics) into solid-state condensed matter systems, providing a concrete transport measurement analogue for studying quantum phase transitions like exciton condensation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Setup and sharp transport signatures of exciton condensation. (a) We consider a multilayer TMD structure; the upper layer is hole-doped, while interlayer excitons are electrically injected between the middle and lower layers. Holes tunnel from the upper to the middle layer and scatter with excitons, forming trions as a stable bound state. A transverse electric field Ez tunes the scattering. Electric contacts with a reservoir induce a field Ex in the upper layer which generates a hole current, and a transverse magnetic field B realizes the Hall effect. (b) When excitons condense, T < T ∗, the hole and trion bands hybridize. The local band curvature defines the local effective mass...

FIG. 2. Hall resistivity ρh xy. (a) Hall resistivity ρh xy as a function of detuning ∆for different values of temperature T at nx = 0.25 · 1012 cm−2 (top) and exciton density nx at 1/T = 6 meV−1 (bottom). The red curve corresponds to the absence of the condensate. (b) Physical picture for kinetics and scattering mechanisms. The momentum-space displacement of each Fermi sea α ∈{e, f} depends on its effective charge ξαe and hence on ∆. Off resonance (top) only one Fermi surface is significantly displaced, while near resonance (bottom) both are. This determines the strength of the interband scattering (represented by the width of the red arrow), which in turn determines the asymmetry of ρh xy...

FIG. 3. Longitudinal resistivity. (a) Longitudinal resis- tivity ρh xx as a function of detuning ∆for different values of temperature T at nx = 0.25 · 1012 cm−2 (top) and exciton density nx at 1/T = 6 meV−1 (bottom). The red curve cor- responds to the absence of the condensate. (b) Half Width at Half Maximum (HWHM) of the longitudinal resistivity (top) and condensate density n0 (bottom). Upon condensation, the phase space available for scattering decreases compared to the non-condensed case (red dashed curve, obtained by taking n0 →0 at fixed nx, T), and the resonance region is narrowed. The cusp in HWHM thus detects exciton condensation.

FIG. 4. Magnetic field dependence of the Hall conduc- tivity σh xy near resonance. (a) Negative Hall conductivity −σh xy of the holes in the presence of an exciton condensate as a function of the magnetic field B for several values of ∆ near resonance. At large B, the negative slope (a hallmark of condensation) disappears, as a consequence of strong magne- toresistive effects. (b) Corresponding ρh xy at fixed B = 0.1 T.
